text: The surgeon forms the Roux limb by dividing the jejunum approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligamentum teres, using surgical instruments to create the necessary separation.
The surgeon fires a stapler over the mesentery to mobilize it, allowing it to reach up to the stomach.
The surgeon initiates a side-to-side anastomosis, positioned ninety centimeters from the cut end of the Roux limb, using surgical instruments to join the intestinal segments.
The surgeon uses an endolinear stapler to create a side-to-side anastomosis, with anti-mesenteric water being taken from the tube.
The surgeon creates an opening with the retrocolic Roux limb for the gastrojejunostomy, preparing for the anastomosis between the stomach and the jejunum.
The surgeon initiates the creation of the gastric pouch by using an endogenous stapler to form a horizontal row, followed by a vertical row using a blue cartridge stapler.
The surgeon begins an end-to-side anastomosis using the endolinear stapler, connecting the gastric pouch to the intestinal limb.
The surgeon fixes the roux limb to the stomach, securing the connection between these structures.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to perform the gastrointestinal anastomosis, completing the connection between the gastric pouch and the intestinal limb.
The surgeon closes the opening of the gastrointestinal tract using sutures, completing the closure of the anastomosis site.
